title: Show HN: Pulpie – Models for Cleaning the Web
content:
The discussion centers on **Pulpie**, an open-source model released by Shreyash of Feyn, designed to strip boilerplate (ads, navigation, footers) from web pages to produce clean text or Markdown for data processing.

### Key Technical Points:
*   **Architecture vs. Competitors:** Unlike traditional "decoder" models that generate text one token at a time (making them memory-bound and expensive), Pulpie is an "encoder" model. It performs a single forward pass over HTML, classifying blocks as content or noise. The founder claims this makes it significantly cheaper and more efficient for large-scale web scraping.
*   **Performance:** The developer asserts that Pulpie outperforms traditional rule-based extractors (like Trafilatura) by approximately 25 F1 points, particularly on complex pages, because it evaluates text semantics rather than relying on HTML tag structure.
*   **Deployment:** The models are available on Hugging Face. The developer notes that for pages requiring JavaScript, users must use a headless browser to render the page before passing the HTML to Pulpie.

### Community Reception & Feedback:
*   **Utility:** Users expressed interest in using Pulpie for various applications, including web-to-kindle apps, bookmark archiving, and preparing data for LLM context windows or speech-to-text tools.
*   **Performance Trade-offs:** Several commenters debated the necessity of a machine learning model versus "old-fashioned" deterministic CSS/jQuery selectors. While some argued that rule-based systems are faster and "good enough," others noted that deterministic methods fail on the diverse, messy "shapes" of the modern web, justifying the move to ML.
*   **Technical Queries:** 
    *   **Hardware:** Questions were raised about running the model on modest hardware (e.g., mini-PCs). The developer indicated that because the model is compute-bound rather than memory-bound and has a relatively small footprint (~210M parameters), it should perform well on consumer hardware and handle quantization (INT8) effectively.
    *   **E-commerce/Edge Cases:** The model appears effective for product scraping (e.g., e-commerce sites) and shows high performance on "hard" benchmarks compared to existing tools.

Overall, the reception was positive, with developers appreciating the shift toward more efficient, encoder-based approaches for high-volume data cleaning.
